worth_it_explanation: Buyer and seat controls decide which demand reaches a publisher's inventory, so a control set that drifted out of date can either let through demand the publisher meant to block or shut out demand the publisher needs for fill. The check is a review of the allowlists, blocklists, and bidder roster a publisher already maintains in Microsoft Monetize, so it costs reading time rather than rebuild time, and the finding points to a concrete control the publisher can adjust and remeasure.
intro: Microsoft Monetize (formerly Xandr Monetize) gives a publisher buyer-level controls that decide which demand sources, bidders, and buyer seats can compete for inventory. Allowlists name the buyers a publisher will transact with, blocklists name the ones it will not, and the bidder roster sets which exchanges and demand partners are connected at all. This subcategory is about whether those controls reflect a deliberate, current decision rather than a list that was set once and left to drift. These checks confirm that the buyer and seat controls are maintained on purpose, and that blocks are scoped tightly enough to keep legitimate demand competing for the impression.

audit_checks.title: Buyer allowlists, blocklists, and the bidder roster are not maintained intentionally
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A buyer control set that is not reviewed drifts away from the publisher's current intent. Blocks placed for a one-time issue stay in place after the cause is gone, bidders added during a test stay connected without anyone judging their value, and seats that should be blocked for brand-safety reasons get missed. The inventory keeps transacting against a roster that reflects past decisions rather than present ones.

educate.base:
Microsoft Monetize sits between a publisher's inventory and the buyers competing for it, so the buyer controls a publisher sets decide which demand is allowed to bid at all. An allowlist names the buyers, seats, or demand partners a publisher will transact with. A blocklist names the ones it will not, whether for a brand-safety reason, a payment dispute, or a quality concern. The bidder roster, the set of exchanges and demand partners connected to the platform, decides which sources can reach the auction in the first place. Each of these is a deliberate choice when it is made.

The problem is that these controls are easy to set and easy to forget. A block added to handle a specific malvertising incident stays in place long after the offending buyer cleaned up its act. A demand partner connected for a short test stays on the roster without anyone judging whether it pays its way. A seat that should be blocked because a buyer moved to a banned category never gets added because no one was watching. None of these failures throws an error. The auction keeps running, and the control set quietly reflects decisions from months ago rather than the publisher's current intent.

The audit question is whether someone owns these controls and reviews them on a schedule, not whether any single entry is right. A control set that is reviewed on a cadence, with a record of why each block exists and when it was last checked, is maintained. A control set that no one can explain, where blocks have no recorded reason and the bidder roster has not been pruned in a year, has drifted.

A few signs point to buyer controls that are not maintained intentionally:
  • Blocks exist with no recorded reason or date, so no one can say whether the cause that prompted them still applies
  • The bidder roster includes demand partners that no one can tie to a current revenue or strategy reason
  • No review cadence exists for the allowlists, blocklists, and roster, and no person owns keeping them current
You may want to confirm that someone owns the buyer controls and reviews them on a set schedule. One approach is to record, for each block and each connected bidder, the reason it exists and the date it was last reviewed, then retire any entry whose reason no longer holds.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site, brand-safety blocks often accumulate around news content, where advertisers pull back from sensitive topics and a publisher may block categories of buyers in response. You may want to review those blocks against current demand because a block placed during one news cycle can suppress fill long after the sensitivity passed.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content site, the buyer roster may include retail-endemic demand partners added to chase higher-intent inventory. You may want to confirm each connected partner still pays its way on the commercial pages because a partner added for a seasonal push can linger on the roster without contributing once the season ends.

investigate.intro: Review the buyer allowlists, blocklists, and the connected bidder roster, and look for a recorded reason and review date behind each entry.

monetize_ui.label: Microsoft Monetize console
steps: Open the buyer and demand controls and list the active allowlists and blocklists with the buyers or seats each one names.
List the connected bidders and demand partners on the roster, and note which ones are actively transacting.
For each block and each connected bidder, record whether a reason and a last-reviewed date exist, or whether the entry is unexplained.
Ask the publisher who owns these controls and on what cadence they are reviewed, and record the answer.

monetize_reporting.label: Microsoft Monetize reporting
steps: Build a report broken out by buyer or demand partner, with bid volume, win rate, and revenue.
Identify connected partners that show little or no bid volume, which points to roster entries that may no longer earn their place.
Note any block whose effect on fill or revenue is large enough to warrant confirming the block is still intended.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records each allowlist, blocklist, and connected bidder alongside whether a reason and a last-reviewed date exist, and who owns the review cadence. A control set with a named owner, a review schedule, and a recorded reason behind each block and connected partner passes. A control set with unexplained blocks, an unpruned roster, and no owner or cadence is a fail recorded with the entries that lack a current justification.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the buyer controls and whether a reason, review date, and owner exist for each above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that the buyer allowlists, blocklists, and bidder roster on {{site_name}} are not reviewed on a schedule, so the control set reflects past decisions rather than current intent. Blocks placed for a one-time issue stay in place after the cause is gone, and demand partners connected for a test linger on the roster without anyone judging their value. {{voice_recommend}} recording the reason and last-reviewed date behind each block and each connected bidder, naming an owner for the controls, and setting a review cadence that retires any entry whose reason no longer holds. After the review, recheck fill and revenue to confirm the controls still match the demand the inventory needs.

audit_checks.title: Buyer or seat blocks are scoped so broadly that they suppress legitimate demand
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A block scoped wider than the problem it was meant to solve shuts out demand the publisher would have wanted. Blocking an entire buyer to handle one bad creative, or blocking a category broad enough to catch good buyers alongside the targeted one, lowers competition in the auction. Fewer bidders means lower clearing prices and lower fill, so an overly broad block costs revenue while it sits unexamined.

educate.base:
A buyer or seat block is a blunt control by design, so the scope a publisher chooses for it matters as much as the decision to block. Microsoft Monetize lets a publisher block at different levels, from a specific creative or advertiser domain up to an entire buyer seat or a whole content category. Blocking narrowly removes the exact demand that caused a problem. Blocking broadly removes that demand and a wide margin around it, including buyers the publisher would happily transact with.

The cost of an overly broad block is paid in the auction. Every buyer a block removes is one fewer bidder competing for the impression. Fewer bidders means less price pressure, which lowers the clearing price, and on inventory where demand is already thin it can drop the impression below the floor so it goes unsold. A block placed to solve a brand-safety or quality problem is doing its job, but if it is scoped to catch ten buyers when one was the issue, the other nine are revenue the publisher gave up without meaning to.

The audit question is whether each block is scoped to the actual problem or whether it reaches wider than it needs to. Standards bodies have built supply-chain tools, e.g., the IAB Tech Lab sellers.json and SupplyChain object, that let a publisher see which entities sit behind a buyer, so a block can target the specific seat or intermediary at fault rather than a broad swath of demand. The narrower the block that solves the problem, the more legitimate demand stays in the auction.

A few signs point to blocks scoped wider than the problem:
  • An entire buyer or seat is blocked when the issue was a single creative or advertiser the platform could have blocked directly
  • A broad content-category block catches buyers the publisher would transact with alongside the ones it meant to exclude
  • Fill rate or clearing price on a slice of inventory dropped after a block was added, with no review of whether the block was scoped too wide
You may want to confirm each block is scoped to the problem it was meant to solve. One approach is to review the blocks against the demand they remove, then narrow any block that excludes more buyers than the issue required.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site, a brand-safety concern on one section can prompt a category-wide block that also applies to sections where the concern does not exist. You may want to scope blocks to the placements or sections that actually raised the concern, so demand keeps competing on the inventory that was never at issue.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content site, a block placed to keep a competitor's ads off product pages can be scoped to the whole account when it only needed to cover the commercial inventory. You may want to confirm the block targets the placements that matter rather than removing the buyer everywhere, which would cut demand on editorial inventory too.

investigate.intro: Review the scope of each active buyer, seat, and category block against the demand it removes, and check fill and clearing price on the affected inventory.

monetize_ui.label: Microsoft Monetize console
steps: Open the buyer and demand controls and list each active block with the level it applies at, e.g., creative, advertiser, seat, or category.
For each block, note how much demand it removes and whether a narrower scope, such as a single creative or advertiser, would have solved the same problem.
Record any block that applies account-wide when the issue it addressed was limited to a section, placement, or single buyer.

monetize_reporting.label: Microsoft Monetize reporting
steps: Build a report broken out by the inventory a block affects, with bid density, fill rate, and average clearing price.
Compare the affected inventory against comparable inventory with no block to see whether the block lowered competition more than the problem justified.
Note any case where fill or clearing price fell after a block was added, which points to a block that may be scoped too wide.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records each block, the level it applies at, and the demand it removes, alongside fill and clearing price on the affected inventory. Blocks scoped to the specific creative, advertiser, or seat at fault, with legitimate demand left competing, pass. A block that removes a broad swath of demand to solve a narrow problem, or one that coincides with a drop in fill or clearing price on the affected inventory, is a fail recorded with the block and the demand it suppressed.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record each block's scope and the fill and clearing price on the affected inventory above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that one or more buyer or seat blocks on {{site_name}} are scoped wider than the problem they were meant to solve, so they remove legitimate demand alongside the targeted buyer. Each buyer a block excludes is one fewer bidder competing for the impression, which lowers clearing prices and can drop thin inventory below the floor so it goes unsold. {{voice_recommend}} reviewing each block against the demand it removes, narrowing any block that excludes more buyers than the issue required, and targeting the specific creative, advertiser, or seat at fault rather than a broad category. After narrowing the blocks, recheck bid density, fill, and clearing price on the affected inventory to confirm the legitimate demand returned.
